informal: Suppose $T \in \mathcal{L}(V)$ is self-adjoint, $\lambda \in \mathbf{F}$, and $\epsilon>0$. Prove that if there exists $v \in V$ such that $\|v\|=1$ and $\|T v-\lambda v\|<\epsilon,$ then $T$ has an eigenvalue $\lambda^{\prime}$ such that $\left|\lambda-\lambda^{\prime}\right|<\epsilon$.
lean:
theorem Axler_exercise_7_14 {𝕜 V : Type*} [RCLike 𝕜] [NormedAddCommGroup V]
  [InnerProductSpace 𝕜 V] [FiniteDimensional 𝕜 V]
  {T : Module.End 𝕜 V} (hT : IsSelfAdjoint T)
  {l : 𝕜} {ε : ℝ} (he : ε > 0) : (∃ v : V, ‖v‖= 1 ∧ ‖T v - l • v‖ < ε) →
  (∃ l' : T.Eigenvalues, ‖l - l'‖ < ε)
reasoning: The Lean signature captures the informal claim accurately: T is a self-adjoint endomorphism of a finite-dimensional inner product space over RCLike 𝕜 (which corresponds to F = ℝ or ℂ in Axler), λ ∈ 𝕜, ε > 0. The hypothesis ∃ v, ‖v‖ = 1 ∧ ‖T v - l • v‖ < ε matches the informal hypothesis exactly. The conclusion ∃ l' : T.Eigenvalues, ‖l - l'‖ < ε uses Mathlib's subtype of eigenvalues of T (those scalars μ with HasEigenvalue), and ‖l - l'‖ in RCLike is the absolute value |λ - λ'|, so this matches "T has an eigenvalue λ' with |λ - λ'| < ε". Finite-dimensionality is implicit in Axler's setup (T ∈ L(V) presupposes finite-dim V in his book), so adding [FiniteDimensional 𝕜 V] is faithful rather than a strengthening or weakening.
